blurb: A soft, airy mound of fine threadlike foliage smothered for months in pale butter-yellow daisies — the 1992 Perennial Plant of the Year, drought-proof, deer-resistant, and a butterfly favorite that blooms summer to frost on the lean Front Range.
spread: Clumping; slowly spreads by rhizome, sterile so it doesn't self-seed
seasons: Ferny threadleaf foliage spring–fall; pale-yellow daisies June–Oct; dies back to ground in winter
wildlife: Butterfly and native-bee nectar plant; songbirds eat coreopsis seed
habitat: Dry open woods, clearings & rocky barrens

care.hardiness: Very cold-hardy — USDA zones 3–9, so it sails through Front Range winters (Boulder is ~5b–6a). 'Moonbeam' was the 1992 Perennial Plant of the Year, chosen for low maintenance, a months-long bloom season, and broad adaptability; it is one of the most dependable, drought-tolerant perennials for a hot, lean spot here.[1][2]
care.planting: Set out nursery plants in spring after the mid-May average last frost, or in early fall, into average to lean, well-drained soil — it actually prefers poor, sandy, or rocky ground over rich beds. In containers it grows well in a fast-draining mix; potted crowns are far less cold-hardy than in-ground ones, so overwinter pots in an unheated garage or sink them in a protected bed.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun — six-plus hours — for the densest mound and heaviest bloom. It tolerates light afternoon shade in the hottest gardens, but too much shade makes it sparse and floppy and cuts the flowering sharply.[1][2]
care.soil: Average to lean, dry-to-medium, well-drained soil; it thrives in poor, sandy, or rocky ground and tolerates Colorado's slightly acid-to-alkaline range. Sharp drainage is the main requirement — rich or wet soil softens the plant and invites crown rot.[1][2]
care.water: Low. Water to establish the first season, then it is notably drought-, heat-, and dry-soil tolerant — give only occasional deep soakings in extended dry heat. It dislikes wet feet, especially over winter.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space plants about 18 in apart; each forms a rounded mound roughly 18–24 in tall and wide that slowly knits together via short rhizomes.[1][2]
care.propagation: 'Moonbeam' is a sterile cultivar, so it is propagated vegetatively, not from seed (seed won't come true and the plant sets little). Divide the clump in spring or fall every 2–3 years to renew vigor and make more plants, or take root cuttings.[1][2]
care.bloom: Masses of pale, creamy-butter-yellow daisies from early summer into fall — roughly June into October on the Front Range. Deadheading or a midsummer shear spurs a strong rebloom and keeps the mound tidy; because it is sterile it stays in flower without self-sowing.[1][2]
care.feeding: Light to none. It flowers best in lean soil; a little balanced slow-release fertilizer in spring is plenty. Avoid rich feeding, which produces lax growth and fewer flowers.[1]
care.maintenance: Shear or deadhead spent flowers in midsummer to trigger a fresh flush and neaten the plant. Cut the whole clump to the ground after frost or in early spring. Divide every 2–3 years in spring or fall to keep the center from thinning out.[1][2]
care.troubles: No serious pests or diseases. Occasional slugs/snails chew foliage, and crown rot develops only in moist, poorly drained soil — sharp drainage and a lean, sunny site prevent it. Powdery mildew can appear in crowded, humid plantings; good airflow avoids it.[1][2]

edible.summary: An ornamental garden perennial with no documented edible use — not notably poisonous, but not a food plant, so don't eat it.[2]
edible.caution: Coreopsis verticillata 'Moonbeam' is grown strictly as an ornamental and pollinator plant. It is rated non-toxic to dogs, cats, and horses, but authoritative sources document no established culinary use or edible part for it — there is no positive food record, so treat it as a non-food plant and do not eat the flowers, foliage, or roots.[2]

gaps: Photos are species-representative Coreopsis verticillata (threadleaf foliage, flowers, seedheads) — no open-licensed photo of the named 'Moonbeam' cultivar exists. 'Moonbeam' is a softer pale butter-yellow than the deeper gold of the species shown; the foliage, habit, and seedheads are identical.
